title: 第 26 節
en: Now it may be quite true that none of these illustrations can be considered, nor were they intended as arguments in support of the Upanishad-philosophy, but they are at all events very useful in reminding us by means of striking similes of certain doctrines arrived at by the Vedanta philosophers in their search after truth.

IT would be interesting to trace at once the same 01 very similar tendencies to those of the Vedanta in the development of other Indian philosophies, and particularly of the Sa?nkhya and Yoga, and to see what they have to say on the existence and the true nature of a Supreme Being, and the relation of human beings to that Divine Being, as shadowed forth in certain passages of the Veda, though differently interpreted by different schools of philosophy. But it seems better on the whole to adhere to the order adopted by the students of philosophy in India, and treat of the other Mimamsa, the Purva-Mimamsa, that is the Former Mimamsa, as it is called, in connection with the one we have examined. The Hindus admit a PurvaMimams& and an Uttara-Mimamsa. They look upon the Ved&nta as the Uttara- or later Mima??isa, and on *that of (raimini as the Purva-, or prior. These names, however, were not meant to imply, as Colebrooke1 seems to have supposed, that the Purva-Mimamsa was prior in time, though it is true that it is sometimes called PraM2, previous. It really meant no more than that the PftrvaMtm&msa, having to do with the Karmak&ntZa, the first or work-part of the Veda, comes first, and the UttaraMfmams&, being concerned with the (?/7anaka7z,cZa, comes second, just as an orthodox Hindu at one time was required to be a Grihastha or householder first, and then only to retire into the forest and lead the contemplative life of a V&naprastha or a Samnyasin. We shall see, however, that this prior Mimamsa, if it can be called a philosophy at all, is very inferior in interest to the VedHnta, 1 Colebrooke, Misc. Essays, vol. i, p. 239, Bitter, History of PMlosophy, vol. iv, p. 376, in Morrison's translation. a Sarvadarsana-sawgraha, p. 122, 1. 3.

and could hardly be understood without the previous existence of such a system as that of Badar&yam. I should not like, however, to commit myself so far as to claim priority in time for the Ved&nta. It has a decided priority in importance, and in its relation to the 6rtf&na-portion of the Veda. We saw why the fact that Badarayarta quotes Gaimini cannot be used for chronological purposes, for Gaimini returns the compliment and quotes BMar&ya72,a. How this is to be accounted for, I tried to explain before. It is clear that while Badarayarca endeavoured to introduce order into the Upanishads, and to reduce their various guesses to something like a system, Craimini undertook to do the same for the rest of the Veda, the so-called Karmak&nda, or work-portion; that is, all that had regard to sacrifice, as described chiefly in the Brahma^as. Sacrifice was so much the daily life of the Br&hmans that the recognised name for sacrifice was simply Karman, i.e. work. That work grew up in different parts of India, just as we saw philosophy springing up, full of variety, not free even from contradictions. Every day had its sacrifice, and in some respects these regular sacrifices may be called the first calendar of India. They depended on the seasons or regulated the seasons and marked the different divisions of the year. There were some rites that lasted the whole year or even several years. And as philosophy existed, independent of the Upanishads, and through Badarayawa attempted to make peace with the Upanishads, we must consider that sacrifices also existed for a long time without the Brahmauas, such as we possess them ; that they grew up without being restrained by generally binding authorities of any kind ; and that at a later time only, after the Brahma?ias had been composed and had acquired some kind of authority, the necessity began to be felt of reconciling variant opinions and customs, as embodied in the BrahmaTzas and elsewhere, giving general as well as special rules for the performance of every kind of ceremony. We can hardly imagine that there ever was a time in India when the so-called priests, settled in distant localities, did not know how to perform their own sacrificial duties, for who were the authors of them, if not the priests ? But PUJRVA-MIMAJfSA, 199 when the Brahmawas once existed, a new problem had to be solved : how to bring the Brahma?ias into harmony with themselves and with existing family and local customs, and also how to discover in them a meaning that should satisfy every new generation. This was achieved by means of what is called Mimamsa, investigation, examination, consideration. There is little room for real philosophy in all this, but there are questions such as that of Dharma or duty, including sacrificial duties, which offer an opportunity for discussing the origin of duty and the nature of its rewards ; while in accounting for seeming contradictions and in arriving at general principles concerning sacrificial acts, problems would naturally turn up which, though often in themselves valueless, are generally treated with considerable ingenuity. In this way the work of Craimini secured for itself a place by the side of the works ascribed to BadarayaTia, Kapila and others, and was actually raised to the rank of one of the six classical philosophies of India. It cannot therefore be passed over in a survey of Indian philosophy.

While BMarayaTia begins his Sutras with Athato Brahmagigw&sa, e Now therefore the desire of knowing Brahman/ Craimini, apparently in imitation of it, begins with Athato Dha:ima#igrn£sa, 'Now therefore the desire of knowing Dharma or duty.' The two words { Now therefore * offer as usual a large scope to a number of interpreters, but they mean no more in the end than that now, after the Veda has been read, and because it has been read, there arises a desire for knowing the full meaning of either Dharma, duty, or of Brahman, the Absolute ; the former treated in the Uttara-, the latter in the Pftrva-Mlm&msa. In fact, whatever native commentators may say to the contrary, this first Sutra is not much more than a title, as if we were to say, Now begins the philosophy of duty, or the philosophy of Saimini.

Dharma, here translated by duty, refers to acts of prescriptive observance, chiefly sacrifices. It is said to be a neuter, if used in the latter sense, a very natural distinction, though there is little evidence to that effect in the Sutras or in the literature known to us.

This Dharma or duty is enjoined in the Brahma^as, and these together with the Mantras are held to constitute the whole of the Veda, so that whatever is not Mantra is Brahma?ia, whatever is not Brahma-na is Mantra. The Brahma?? as are said to consist of Vidhis, injunctions, and ArthavMas, glosses. The injunctions are meant either to make us do a thing that had not been done before, or to make us know a thing that had not been known before \ Subsequently the Vidhis2 are divided into Utpatti-vidhis, original or general injunctions, such as Agnihotra?)i cyuhoti, he performs the Agnihotra, and Viniyoga-vidhi, showing the manner iu which a sacrifice is to be performed. The latter comprises injunctions as to the details, such as Dadhna $ulioti, he performs the sacrifice with sour milk, &c. Then follow the Prayoga-vidhis which settle the exact order of sacrificial performances, and there is lastly a class of injunctions which determine who is fit to perform a sacrificial act. They are called Adhikara-vidhis.

The hymns or formulas which are to be used at a sacrifice, though they are held to possess also a transcendental or mysterious effect, the Apftrva, are conceived by Gainiini as mainly intended to remind the sacrificer of the gods who are to receive his sacrificial gifts.

He likewise lays stress on what he calls Namadheya or the technical name of each sacrifice, such as Agnihotra, Darsapur?iam&sa, Udbhid, &c* These names are found in the Brahma9ias, and they are considered important, as no doubt they are, in defining the nature of a sacrifice. The Nishedhas or prohibitions require no explanation. They simply state what ought not to be done at a sacrifice.
Lastly, the Arthav&das are passages in the Br&hmawas which explain certain things ; they vary in character, being either glosses, comments, or explanatory statements.
Contents of tke Pftrva-Mim&ms&.
Perhaps I cannot do better than give the principal contents of ffaimini's Sfltras, as detailed by M&dhava in his 1 Eigvedabliashya, vol. i, p. 5.
2 Thibaut, ArthasaMgraha, p. viii.

CONTENTS OE THE PUKVA-MIMAlfSA. 2OI Nyaya-mala-vistara l. The Himamsa consists of twelve books. In the first book is discussed the authoritativeness of those collections of words which are severally meant by the terms injunction (Vidhi), explanatory passage (Arthavada), hymn (Mantra), tradition (Smnti), and name (Namadheya). In the second we find certain subsidiary discussions, as e. g. on Apurva, relative to the difference of various rites, refutation of erroneously alleged proofs, and difference of performance, as in obligatory and voluntary offerings. In the third are considered revelation (Sruti), ' sign ' or sense of a passage (Linga), ' context ' (Vakya), Sec., and their respective weight, when in apparent opposition to one another; then the ceremonies called Pratipathi-Karmam, things mentioned by the way, Anarabhyadhita, things accessory to several main objects, as Praya</as, &c., and the duties of the sacrificer. In the fourth the chief subject is the influence of the principal and subordinate rites on other rites, the fruit produced by the ffuliu when made of the Butea frondosa, &c.3 and the dice-playing, &c., which forms part of the Ra^asuya-sacrifice. In the fifth the subjects are the relative order of different passages of the /Sruti, &e., the order of different parts of a sacrifice, as the seventeen animals at the Vlk/apeya, the multiplication and non-multiplication of rites, and the respective force of the words of the $ruti, the order of mention, &c., as determining the order of performance. In the sixth we read of the persons qualified to offer sacrifices, their obligations, the substitutes for prescribed materials, supplies for lost or injured offerings, expiatory rites, the Sattra-offerings, things proper to be given, and the different sacrificial fines. In the seventh is treated the mode of transference of the ceremonies of one sacrifice to another by direct command in the Yaidie text, others as inferred by 'name' or csign.J In the eighth, transference by virtue of the clearly expressed or obscurely expressed c sign ' or by the predominant c sign/ and cases also where no transference takes place. In the ninth, the discussion begins with the adaptation (uha) of hymns, 1 See Cowell and G-ough in their translation of the Sarradarsanasawgraha, p. 178.

when quoted in a new connection, the adaptation of S&mans and Mantras, and collateral questions connected therewith. In the tenth the occasions are discussed where the nonperformance of the primary rite involves the 'preclusion' and non-performance of the dependent rites, and occasions when rites are precluded, because other rites produce their special results, also Graha-offerings, certain Samans, and various other things, as well as different kinds of negation. In the eleventh we find the incidental mention and subsequently the fuller discussion of Tantra, where several acts are combined into one, and Avapa, or the performing an act more than once. In the twelfth there is the discussion on Prasanga, when the rite is performed with one chief purpose, but with an incidental further reference, on Tantra, cumulation of concurrent rites (Samu/cMaya), and option.

It is easy to see from this table of contents that neither Plato nor Kant would have felt much the wiser for them. But we must take philosophies as they are given us ; and we should spoil the picture of the philosophical life of India, if we left out of consideration their speculations about sacrifice as contained in the Purva-MimamsL There are passages, however, which appeal to philosophers, such as, for instance, the chapter on the Pram^as or the authoritative sources of knowledge, on the relation between word and thought, and similar subjects. It is true that most of these questions are treated in the other philosophies also, but they have a peculiar interest as treated by the ritualistic Fram&nas of 6raimini.

Thus if we turn our attention first to the Pram&^as, the measures of knowledge, or the authorities to which we can appeal as the legitimate means of knowledge, as explained by the Purva-Mimams&, we saw before that the Ved&ntists did not pay much attention to them, though they were acquainted with the three fundamental Pram^as — senseperception, inference, and revelation. The Purva-Mim&ws&, oil the contrary, devoted considerable attention to this subject, and admitted five, (i) Sense-perception, Pratyaksha, SUTBA-STYLE. 203 when the organs are actually in contiguity with an object , (2) Inference (Anumana), i. e> the apprehension of an unseen member of a known association (Vyapti) by the perception of another seen member; (3) Comparison (Upamana), knowledge arising from resemblance; (4) Presumption (Arthapatti), such knowledge as can be derived of a thing not itself perceived, but implied by another ; (5) /Sabda, verbal information derived from authoritative sources. One sect of Mimamsakas, those who follow KumarilaBhafta, admitted besides, (6) Abhava, not-being, which seems but a subdivision of inference, as if we infer dryness of the soil from the notbeing or absence of clouds and rain.

All these sources of information are carefully examined, but it is curious that Mimamsakas should admit this large array of sources t of valid cognition, considering that for their own purposes, for establishing the nature of Dharma or duty, they practically admit but one, namely scripture or $abda. Duty, they hold, cannot rest on human authority, because the ' ought ' which underlies all duty, can only be supplied by an authority that is more than human or more than fallible, and such an authority is nowhere to be found except in the Veda. This leaves, of course, the task of proving the superhuman origin of the Veda on the shoulders of ffaimini ; and we shall see hereafter how he performs it.
Stitra-style.

Before, however, we enter on a consideration of any of the problems treated in the Purva-Mimamsa, a few remarks have to be made on a peculiarity in the structure of the Sutras. In order to discuss a subject fully, and to arrive in the end at a definite opinion, the authors 'of the Sutras are encouraged to begin with stating first every possible objection that can reasonably be urged against what is their own opinion. As long as the objections are not perfectly absurd, they have a riglit to be stated, and this is called the Purvapaksha, the first part. Then follow answers to all these objections, and this is called the Uttarapaksha, the latter part ; and then only are we led on to the final conclusion, the Siddhanta. This system is exhaustive and 204 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

has many advantages, ,but it has also the disadvantage, as far as the reader is concerned, that, without a commentary, he often feels doubtful where the cons end and the pros begin. The commentators themselves differ sometimes on that point. Sometimes again, instead of three, a case or Adhikara^a is stated in five members, namely : —
1. The subject to be explained (Vishaya).
2. The doubt (Samsaya).
3. The first side or prima facie view (Purvapaksha).
4. The demonstrated conclusion (Sidclhanta) ; and 5. The connection (Samgati).

This is illustrated in the commentary on the first and second Sutras of the Mirnamsa 1? which declares that a desire to know duty is to be entertained, and then defines duty (Dharma) as that which is to be recognised by an instigatory passage, that is by a passage from the Veda. Here the question to be discussed (Vishaya) is, whether the study of Duty in (rainnni's Mima?7isa is really necessary to be undertaken. The Piirvapaksha says of course, No, for when it is said that the Veda should be learnt (Vedo understood, like any other book which we read, or that it should be learnt by heart without any attempt, as yet, on the part of the pupil to understand it, simply as a work good in itself, which has its reward in heaven. This is a very common view among the ancient Brahmans ; for, as they had no written books, they had a very perfect system for imprinting tests on the memory of young persons, by making them learn every day a certain number of verses or lines by heart, without any attempt, at first, of making them understand what they learnt; and afterwards only supplying the -key to the meaning. This acquisition of the mere sound of the Veda was considered highly meritorious ; nay, some held that the Veda was more efficacious, if not understood than if understood. This was in fact their printing or rather their writing, and without it their mnemonic literature would have been simply impossible.
1 Sarvadarsana-sawgraha, p. 122 • translation by Cowell and Gough, p. 180 } SiddMnta Biplka, 1898, p. 194.

SUTRA-STYLE. 205 As we warn our compositors against trying to understand what they are printing, Indian pupils were cautioned against the same danger ; and they succeeded in learning the longest texts by heart, without even attempting at first to fathom their meaning. To us such a system seems almost incredible, but no other system was possible in ancient times, and there is no excuse for being incredulous, for it may still be witnessed in India to the present day.

Only after the text had thus been imprinted on the memory, there came the necessity of interpretation or understanding. And here the more enlightened of the Indian theologians argue that the Vedic command c Vedo acquired, to be learnt by heart,3 implies that it is also to be understood, and that this intelligible purpose is preferable to the purely mechanical one, though miraculous rewards may be held out for that.

But if so, it is asked, what can be the use of the Mimamsa? The pupil learns the Veda by heart, and learns to understand it in the house of his teacher. After that he bathes, marries and sets up his own house, so that it is argued there would actually be no time for any intervening study of the Himamsa. Therefore the imaginary opponent, the Purvapakshin, objects that the study of the Mlmamsa is not necessary at all, considering that it rests on no definite sacred command. But here the Siddhantin steps forward and says that the Snmti passage enjoining a pupil's bathing (graduating) on returning to his house is not violated by an intervening study of the Mimamsa, because it is not said that, after having finished his apprenticeship, he should immediately bathe ; and because, though his learning of the text of the Veda is useful in every respect, a more minute study of the sacrificial precepts of the Veda, such as is given in the Mimamsa, cannot be considered superfluous, as a means towards the highest object of the study of the Veda, viz. the proper performance of its commands.
These considerations in support of the Siddhanta or final conclusion would probably fall under the name of Samgati, connection, though I must confess that its meaning is not 206 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
zh: 現在，雖然這些例子都不能被視為——也並非旨在成為——支持奧義書哲學的論證，但無論如何，它們都極其有用，能夠藉由令人印象深刻的比喻提醒我們那些韋丹塔（Vedanta）哲學家在追求真理的過程中所達成的某些教說。

追溯同樣或相似的傾向在其他印度哲學發展中的表現，特別是在數論派（Sāṃkhya）和瑜伽派中的表現，並看看它們對至高無上的存有的存在與真實本質，以及人類存有與該神聖存有的關係有何見解，這些關係在吠陀的某些篇章中有所暗示，儘管不同的哲學流派對其有不同的詮釋，這固然令人有興趣。然而，似乎更好的做法是遵循印度哲學研究者所採取的順序，將其他的彌曼差派，即所謂的前彌曼差派（Purva-Mimamsa），即前期彌曼差派，與我們已考察過的派別連同處理。印度人承認前彌曼差派（Purva-Mimamsa）與後彌曼差派（Uttara-Mimamsa）。他們將吠檀多視為後期的彌曼差派（Uttara- 或 later Mimamsa），將遮梨摩尼（Jaimini）的派別視為前期的或先前的（Purva-）。然而，這些名稱並非如科爾布魯克所似乎認為的那樣，意味著前彌曼差派在時間上是先的，儘管有時它確實被稱為Pra（先前的）。它實際上的意思無非是說，前彌曼差派因涉及吠陀的業部（Karmakanda），即吠陀的第一部或作業部分，故而排在先；而後彌曼差派因關涉知識部（Jnanakanda），故而排在後，正如一位正統的印度教徒一度被要求首先成為家居者（Grihastha），然後才退入森林，過著林棲者（Vanaprastha）或遊行僧（Samnyasin）的冥想生活一樣。然而，我們將看到，這一在先的彌曼差派，即便可以稱之為哲學，其趣味性也遠遠不如吠檀多。

並且如果沒有巴達拉耶那（Bādarāyaṇa）這樣一個系統的預先存在，幾乎就無法被理解。然而，我不想過分地聲稱吠檀多（Vedānta）在時間上具有優先性。它在重要性上確實具有優先性，並且在其與吠陀的知識部分（jñāna-portion）的關係上也是如此。我們已看到為何巴達拉耶那引述耆摩尼（Jaimini）這一事實無法用於年代學目的，因為耆摩尼也以牙還牙地引述巴達拉耶那。我曾試圖在此前解釋這是如何解釋的。顯然，巴達拉耶那致力於在奧義書中引入秩序，並將其各種推測化為某種體系，而耆摩尼則承擔了為吠陀其餘部分做同樣事情的任務，即所謂的業契（Karmakāṇḍa），或行為部分；也就是說，所有與祭祀相關的內容，主要如梵書（Brāhmaṇas）中所描述的那樣。祭祀在婆羅門的日常生活中佔據如此重要的地位，以至於祭祀的公認名稱就是業（Karman），即工作。該工作在印度的不同地區發展起來，正如我們看到的哲學的萌生一樣，充滿了多樣性，甚至不能免於矛盾。每天都有其祭祀，在某些方面，這些定期的祭祀可以稱為印度的第一份日曆。它們取決於季節或調節季節，並標記了一年的不同部分。有些儀式持續整整一年，甚至好幾年。既然哲學獨立於奧義書而存在，並通過巴達拉耶那試圖與奧義書達成和解，我們必須認為祭祀也獨立於我們所擁有的梵書而長期存在；它們在沒有任何普遍約束性權威制約的情況下成長；只有在後來，在梵書被編纂並獲得了某種權威之後，人們才開始感受到調和梵書及其他地方所體現的不同意見和習俗的必要性，為每一種儀式的執行提供一般性和特殊性的規則。我們幾乎無法想象在印度曾經有過一個時代，當時所謂的祭司，定居在遠方的地點，不知道如何執行他們自己的祭祀職責，因為誰是他們的作者呢，除了祭司外？但是，一旦梵書存在了，一個新的問題就必須得到解決：如何使梵書彼此相和諧，並與現存的家族和地方習俗相和諧，同時也如何在其中發掘一種能滿足每一新世代的意義。這通過所謂的密摩差（Mīmāṃsā），即調查、檢視、思考而被實現。在所有這些中，真正的哲學地位很小，但諸如達摩（Dharma）或職責之類的問題，包括祭祀職責，提供了討論職責起源及其報酬性質的機會；而在說明表面上的矛盾時，以及在達成有關祭祀行為的一般原則時，問題自然會湧現，這些問題雖然本身往往無足輕重，但通常都是以相當的巧思來討論的。通過這種方式，耆摩尼的著作為自己在巴達拉耶那、卡比拉（Kapila）及其他人所著著作之旁邊的位置奠定了基礎，並實際上被提升到印度六大古典哲學之一的地位。因此，在印度哲學的概覽中，它不可被忽視。

當婆羅浮笈多（Bādarāyaṇa）以《梵經》（*Brahma-sūtra*）開篇時，以「阿他多梵摩吉吉莎」（*Athato Brahmagigñāsa*，「現在因此產生對梵的欲求」）為首句，遮黎尼（Jaimini）顯然是對其的模仿，則以「阿他多達摩吉吉莎」（*Athato Dharmagigñāsa*，「現在因此產生對法或責任的欲求」）開始。這兩個詞「現在因此」（*Now therefore*）如常例一樣給予眾多詮釋者以廣大的詮解空間，但最終它們的含義無非是說，現在，在吠陀已被誦讀之後，並且正因為它已被誦讀，便產生了想要認識法（*Dharma*）──責任──或梵（*Brahman*）──絕對者──之完整義涵的欲求；前者在《後密摩曼薩》（*Uttara-Mīmāṃsā*）中論述，後者在《前密摩曼薩》（*Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā*）中論述。事實上，無論本土註疏家如何聲言相反，這第一條經文無非就是一個標題，就好似我們所說的「現在開始論述責任的哲學」，或「現在開始論述遮黎尼的哲學」一樣。
法（Dharma）在此譯為「責任」或「義務」，係指規範性儀式行為，主要為祭祀活動。據稱若用後一種意義，應為中性詞；這雖然是非常自然的區別，但在《經書》（Sutras）或吾人所知的文獻中幾乎沒有證據支持此說。

這種法（Dharma）或義務在梵書（Brāhmana）中被規定，這些梵書與頌文（Mantra）一起被認為構成了吠陀的全部，因此凡非頌文的就是梵書，凡非梵書的就是頌文。梵書據稱由律儀（Vidhi）——即規範——與義疏（Arthavāda）——即注釋——構成。這些規範的目的是要麼使我們做一件前所未有的事，要麼使我們認識一件前所不知的事。隨後，律儀被分為烏特帕迪律儀（Utpatti-vidhi）——原始的或一般的規範，如「阿格尼霍特拉·詩歌霍迪」（*Agnihotra cyuhoti*）「他進行阿格尼霍特拉祭」——與毗尼約迦律儀（Viniyoga-vidhi），說明祭祀應當如何進行。後者包括關於細節的規範，如「達德納·舒利歐迪」（*Dadhna śulioti*）「他用酸奶進行祭祀」，以此類推。隨後是普拉約迦律儀（Prayoga-vidhi），它規定了祭祀行為的確切順序，最後還有一類規範，用以決定誰有資格進行祭祀行為。它們被稱為阿地卡拉律儀（Adhikara-vidhi）。
在祭祀時使用的讚歌或咒語，雖然被認為也具有超越的或神祕的效力，即所謂的阿普瓦（Apftrva），但根據蓋尼尼的理解，其主要目的是提醒祭祀者銘記那些將接受其祭祀禮物的諸神。
他同樣強調了他所謂的「名稱」(Namadheya)，即每一項祭祀的技術性名稱，如阿格尼霍特拉祭（Agnihotra）、達爾沙普爾納祭（Darsapurnamsa）、烏德必德祭（Udbhid）等等。這些名稱見於《梵書》(Brahmanas)，它們被視為重要的，誠然如此，因為它們有助於界定祭祀的性質。《尼舍達》(Nishedhas)，或稱禁止條款，無需詳加說明。它們單純地陳述在祭祀中何者不應為之。
最後，《阿闥婆吠陀》（Arthavdas）是《梵書》（Brâhmanas）中的段落，用以解釋某些事物；其性質多樣，可以是註釋、評論或解釋性陳述。
《前祀經疏》的內容
或許我無法做得更好，只能將艾馬里尼（ffaimini）《經典》（Sfltras）的主要內容轉述出來，如同馬德哈瓦在其《梨俱吠陀注疏》第一卷第五頁中所詳述的那樣。
2 蒂博，《經濟論摘要》(Arthasaśāstra)，第八頁。

《彌麼罕薩》(Mimansa)由十二部書組成。第一部書討論那些各自由「祭祀規範」(Vidhi)、「闡釋段落」(Arthavada)、「讚頌詞」(Mantra)、「傳統」(Smriti)及「名稱」(Namadheya)等術語所指涉的詞彙集合的權威性。第二部則涉及若干附屬性的討論，如關於「阿普爾瓦」(Apurva)、各種祭祀儀式的差異、錯誤聲稱的證據的駁斥，以及義務性與自願性供奉等祭祀執行方式的差異。第三部考察「啟示」(Sruti)、段落的「標記」或「意義」(Linga)、「經文脈絡」(Vakya)等及其各自的權重，當它們顯現為相互對立時；其次是稱為「普拉蒂帕提─卡爾瑪」(Pratipathi-Karmam)的儀式，即順便提及的事項、「未予著手的事物」(Anarabhyadhita)、附屬於多個主要目的的事物(如「普拉亞」(Prayas)等)，以及祭祀者的職責。第四部的主要題目是主要儀式與附屬儀式對其他儀式的影響、以「巴都泰亞」(Butea frondosa)樹製成的供物所產生的果報等，以及構成「羅闍蘇亞祭」(Rajasuya-sacrifice)一部分的掷骰子遊戲等。第五部的題目為《啟示》(Sruti)中不同段落的相對順序等、祭祀不同部分的順序，如「毘克亞帕亞祭」(Vikaapeya)中的十七隻動物、儀式的增乘與非增乘，以及《啟示》詞彙的各自效力、提及的順序等作為決定執行順序的因素。第六部論述有資格進行祭祀的人、其義務、為規定材料所提供的替代物、針對遺失或受損供物的補償、贖罪儀式、「薩特拉供奉」(Sattra-offerings)、適於給予的事物，以及不同的祭祀罰金。第七部論述透過吠陀經文中明確的指令將一項祭祀的儀式轉移到另一項祭祀的方式，或作為透過「名稱」或「標記」推斷的方式。第八部論述透過明確表達或隱晦表達的「標記」或透過優勢性「標記」的轉移，以及亦涉及不發生轉移的情況。第九部以讚頌詞的「適應」(uha)的討論開始，

當在新的脈絡中被引用時，頌讚文（Sāman）與心咒（Mantra）的適應，以及與之相關的附帶問題。在第十篇中討論的是，不執行主要儀式會導致從屬儀式的「排斥」與不執行的情況，以及因為其他儀式產生其特殊結果而儀式被排斥的情況，同時也包括掬食祭祀（Graha-offering）、某些頌讚文（Sāman），以及各種其他事項，以及不同種類的否定。在第十一篇中，我們發現了對密集儀軌（Tantra）的附帶性提及，隨後有更充分的討論，其中多個行為被結合為一體，以及重行（Avapa），即執行一個行為多於一次的情況。在第十二篇中，有關於結合性（Prasanga）的討論，即當儀式以一個主要目的執行，但附帶有進一步的參考時，論及密集儀軌（Tantra）、並行儀式的累積（Samuccaya），以及選擇性。

從這份目錄不難看出，無論柏拉圖還是康德都不會因此感到智慧大增。但我們必須以現有的形式接納各種哲學；如果我們忽視《前彌摩遮》（Purva-Mim^ams^a）中關於祭祀的思辨，就會破壞印度哲學生活的圖景。然而，有些段落對哲學家具有吸引力，例如關於量（Pram^as）或知識的權威性來源的章節，以及論述詞與思想之間的關係和類似主題的章節。誠然，這些問題中的大多數在其他哲學中也有論述，但由沙瑪維尼（Sraimini）的儀式主義彌摩遮派所闡釋的這些問題具有獨特的意義。

因此，如果我們首先將注意力轉向**量度**（Pram&s），即知識的量度，或我們可以訴諸的作為知識之合法手段的權威——如前派密末奢派（Purva-Mimams&）所闡述的——我們之前曾見到，韋檀多派（Vedantists）雖然對三個基本量度有所認識——感官知覺、推理與啟示——卻未曾對其投以過多關注。前派密末奢派（Purva-Mim&ws&）則恰恰相反，對此課題投注了相當的注意力，並承認五種量度：（1）感官知覺（Pratyaksha），當感知器官與對象實際相接觸時；（2）推理（Anumana），即藉由已知關聯（Vyapti）中一個可見成員的知覺而對未見成員的領會；（3）比較（Upamana），源於相似性的知識；（4）推定（Arthapatti），關於某物本身未被知覺但由另一事物所蘊含的知識；（5）言語（Sabda），源於權威來源的語言信息。追隨庫馬里拉·跋多（KumarilaBhafta）的密末奢派的一個宗派，另外還承認（6）非有（Abhava），非存在，這似乎僅為推理的一個分支——如同我們由雲與雨的不存在或缺失而推斷土壤的乾燥一樣。

所有這些資訊來源都經過仔細的檢驗，但值得注意的是，彌曼差派（Mimamsakas）竟然承認這一大套有效認知的來源，考慮到他們自身的目的——建立法（Dharma）或責任的性質——他們實際上只承認一個來源，即經典或聲音（śabda）。他們主張，責任不能建立在人的權威之上，因為貫穿所有責任之下的「應然」（the 'ought'），只能由超越人性或超越謬誤的權威來提供，而這樣的權威除了吠陀之外，別無他處可尋。這當然就把證明吠陀超人性來源的任務留給了迦摩尼（Jaimini）；我們將在後文看到他如何完成這一任務。
我準備好翻譯了，但我發現英文原文只有「Stitra-style.」這短短一句，且內容不完整或有誤。 這似乎不是《宗教學導論》中的標準文本片段。請您確認： 1. 原文是否完整？ 2. 是否應為其他詞彙（如 Sutra-style、Sûtra-style 等）？ 3. 上下文語境為何？ 待您提供完整、核實無誤的英文原文後，我將依循您的規則提供精準的繁體中文翻譯。

在我們著手討論《前彌曼差》(Purva-Mimamsa) 中所涉及的任何問題之前，須先針對經文 (Sutras) 結構中的一項特殊性做若干說明。為了充分討論某一課題，並最終得出確定的見解，《經文》的作者們被鼓勵採取以下做法：首先陳述所有可能合理地針對其自身觀點提出的反對意見。只要這些反對意見不至於完全荒謬，就有權被陳述出來，這稱為前說 (Purvapaksha)，即第一部分。其次是對所有這些反對意見的答辯，這稱為後說 (Uttarapaksha)，即後一部分；唯有如此，我們才得以進入最終的結論，即確定論 (Siddhanta)。此一系統極為詳盡周密。
具有許多優點，但就讀者而言，它也有缺點，即沒有評註，讀者常常對於反方論點與正方論點的分界線在何處感到困惑。評註者本身有時在這一點上也有分歧。有時候，案例或部分（Adhikara）不是以三個成員陳述，而是以五個成員陳述，即：——
待解釋的主題（Vishaya）。
3. 首先的層面或表面上的觀點（Purvapaksha）。
4. 已證明的結論（Siddhânta）；及 5. 關聯性（Samgati）。

這一點在《彌邁昂薩》(Mīmāṃsā)首兩條經文(Sutra)的註疏中得到說明，該註疏宣稱應當培養對於認識義務的欲求，隨後將義務(法，Dharma)定義為應當由激發性段落(即來自吠陀的段落)所認可的事物。此處待討論的問題(論域，Vishaya)是：在羅摩努闍的《彌邁昂薩》中研習義務是否真正必要進行。先行派(Pūrvapaksha)當然說不必要，因為當說吠陀應當被學習時，吠陀應被理解為如同任何其他我們所閱讀的書籍一樣被學習，或者應當被學生背誦而不做任何理解的嘗試，單純作為一部本身具有價值、其獎賞在於天堂的著作。這在古代婆羅門中是一種極為普遍的觀點；因為他們沒有書寫的書籍，他們擁有一套極為完善的系統，用以將經文銘刻在年輕人的記憶中，讓他們每天背誦一定數量的詩句或行文，起初完全不嘗試使其理解所學內容，之後才提供意義的鑰匙。這種對於吠陀純粹聲音的習得被視為極其有功德的；甚至有人主張，吠陀若未被理解反而比被理解時更具效力。這實際上是他們的印刷術或毋寧說是他們的書寫術，沒有它，他們的記憶文學將根本不可能存在。
1 《諸派要義集》(Sarvadarsana-sawgraha)，第 122 頁；柯威爾與戈夫譯本，第 180 頁；《實理明燈》(Siddhānta Bindhu)，1898 年版，第 194 頁。
蘇特拉式。205 正如我們警告排版工人不要試圖理解他們正在印刷的內容，古代印度學生也受到同樣的告誡；他們成功地將最長的經文背得爛熟，最初甚至不曾試圖探究其含義。對我們而言，這樣的制度似乎近乎不可信，但在古代並無其他可行的制度，而且我們也無理由懷疑，因為在今日的印度仍然可以目睹這樣的情形。
只有當經文以這種方式銘刻於記憶之後，才產生了詮釋或理解的必要性。在此，較具啟蒙思想的印度神學家辯稱，吠陀的戒律——「應當誦習吠陀」（*Vedo acquired*）——蘊含著它也應當被理解，而這種理智的目的乃是優於純粹機械性的目的，儘管人們可能期待後者會帶來神蹟的獎賞。

但如果情況如此，人們會問，《彌曼差》的用途何在？弟子在師傅的家中背誦《吠陀》，學習理解它。之後他沐浴、結婚並建立自己的家庭，因此有人辯稱實際上將沒有時間進行任何插入的《彌曼差》研究。因此虛擬的對方——前派論者（Purvapakshin）——反對認為《彌曼差》的研究根本不必要，考慮到它並不奠基於任何確定的聖典命令。但在此時結論論者（Siddhantin）挺身而出，主張關於弟子返回其家後沐浴（授業）的《識論》（Smṛti）經文，並不被《彌曼差》的插入研究所違反，因為並未明確規定在完成其學徒身分後應當立即沐浴；而且，儘管他對《吠陀》經文的學習在各個方面都是有用的，但對《吠陀》祭祀戒律的更為細緻的研究——如《彌曼差》所提供的那樣——不能被視為多餘，因為它是達致《吠陀》研究的最高目標的手段，即正確執行其命令。
這些支持悉檀多（Siddhanta）或最終結論的考量，或許應當歸類於三相（Samgati）——即連貫性之下，儘管我必須坦言其意涵並不明確。
